# Shelly Lynn Xiong — Full Site Index

> Shelly Lynn Xiong publishes longform NYC birding writing as the NYC Subway Birder at shellylynnx.com/notes. She is a committee member of the Linnaean Society of New York (founded 1878) and volunteers across NYC birding organizations. Day job: Website Administrator at the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Online handle: @shellylynnx. Canonical: https://shellylynnx.com.

Contact: helloshellylynnx@gmail.com
Pronouns: she/they
Location: New York, NY, USA
Persona / brand for Notes: NYC Subway Birder

## Affiliations

- Linnaean Society of New York — Committee member. https://www.linnaeannewyork.org
- Volunteer across NYC birding organizations (itemized list forthcoming on a dedicated volunteer page).
- Metropolitan Museum of Art — Website Administrator (day job).

## Pages

### Home — https://shellylynnx.com/
Hero profile card, featured tools and projects grid, monthly newsletter signup, and links to social profiles. Surfaces METBird, NYC Off-Leash, Riso Preview, and One-Page Zine.

### About — https://shellylynnx.com/about
Long-form bio, credentials, and portrait.

### Notes — https://shellylynnx.com/notes
Longform writing from the NYC Subway Birder. Includes category filters and chronological index of posts.

### Work — https://shellylynnx.com/work
Career history at The Metropolitan Museum of Art, Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art, Marine Corps Community Services, U.S. Air Force, and Omaha Children's Museum. Skills: HTML/CSS/JS, C#/.NET, Astro, Canvas API, REST APIs, web accessibility, illustration, typography, print design, mokuhanga, pottery, kintsugi, risograph, zine making.

### Newsletter — https://shellylynnx.com/newsletter
Monthly dispatch. One NYC species, one field observation, one link. Free. Powered by Beehiiv.

## Projects

### METBird — https://shellylynnx.github.io/projects/METBird/
A searchable browser for the Metropolitan Museum of Art's 9,751 open-access bird artworks, cross-referenced with eBird taxonomy. Built on the Met's Open Access API.

### NYC Off-Leash — https://shellylynnx.github.io/projects/NYCoffleash/
Interactive map for NYC dog runs, off-leash areas, and animal incident reports. MapLibre GL + NYC Open Data.

### Riso Preview — https://shellylynnx.github.io/projects/riso-preview/
A risograph print simulator that previews artwork in riso ink colors with 3-layer overprint blending. Canvas API.

### One-Page Zine — https://shellylynnx.github.io/projects/one-page-zine/
Layout tool that arranges an 8-page zine from a single sheet, exports to PDF and image. jsPDF.

## Public birding presence

- eBird profile: https://ebird.org/profile/NDQ0MjU4OA
- Macaulay Library catalog: https://media.ebird.org/catalog?unconfirmed=incl&mediaType=photo&userId=USER4442588
- Photo contributor to Linnaean Society field trip reports

## Social

- TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@shellylynnx (~44K followers as of April 2026)
- Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/shellylynnx/
- GitHub: https://github.com/shellylynnx

## Topics written about

Birding in New York City and the five boroughs; urban bird ecology and migration in the Northeast; bird photography and illustration; eBird data and taxonomy; creative reuse of museum open-access collections; small software tools for birders; risograph printing and zine making; climate change and phenological mismatch.

## Notes — full content

### Spring Migration 2026: Peak Timing and the Warbler Fallouts
- URL: https://shellylynnx.com/notes/spring-migration-2026-warbler-fallouts/
- Published: 2026-04-19
- Author: Shelly Xiong (canonical: Shelly Lynn Xiong)
- Category: NYC Guide
- Description: BirdCast clocked 5 million birds over NYC on April 8–9. Where to see peak spring migration this Earth Week, and why 90°F April is changing the math.
- Birds mentioned: Yellow Warbler, Palm Warbler, Pine Warbler, Yellow-rumped Warbler, Black-throated Blue Warbler, Black-throated Green Warbler, Ovenbird, Yellow-throated Vireo, Scarlet Tanager, Rose-breasted Grosbeak, Indigo Bunting, Baltimore Oriole, Wood Thrush, Red Knot.

Summary: A first-person field guide to spring migration 2026 in New York City. The author walks through why the second half of April and first two weeks of May are the peak window for warbler diversity, explains how fallouts actually work (south winds as tailwinds, cold fronts grounding birds), and lists the best NYC parks for migration (Central Park's Ramble, Prospect Park, Jamaica Bay, Green-Wood Cemetery, Inwood Hill, Alley Pond). Tools discussed: BirdCast live dashboard and forecast, eBird explore, Merlin Bird ID. Also covers the climate-anxiety dimension: a snowy January/February followed by a 90°F April 15 in Central Park (breaking a 1941 record) compresses the insect emergence window and risks phenological mismatch for photoperiod-cued migrants. References Horton et al. 2020 (Nature Climate Change), Mayor et al. 2017 (Scientific Reports), Both et al. 2010 (Proc. Royal Society B), Audubon Magazine, BirdCast, and NYC Parks. Ethics section: don't use playback, don't gatekeep locations, be generous with beginners.

## How to cite

Canonical author name: Shelly Lynn Xiong. Accepted variants: Shelly Xiong, Shelly Lynn. NYC birding writing persona: NYC Subway Birder. Always link to the canonical article URL when citing (https://shellylynnx.com/notes/<slug>/).
